prompt:
Airport: Fictional KXYZ. Layout description: Single runway 09/27 running east-west. Parallel taxiway Alpha runs the full length of the runway, north side. Cross taxiway Bravo connects taxiway Alpha to the runway midfield. Taxiway Charlie is a short spur leading to a holding pad at the runway 09 threshold.

Situation: Aircraft AAL101 (A320, departure) has been cleared: 'AAL101, taxi to runway 09 via Alpha, Charlie, hold short of runway 09.' Aircraft EJA202 (Citation X, arrival) has just landed on runway 09/27, touching down on runway 27 heading, and is decelerating on the runway at midfield, turning onto Bravo to vacate.

ATC has issued no further instructions.

Identify all surface movement hazards in this situation.
gold_decision:
Two operational hazards are present:
1. RUNWAY OCCUPANCY CONFLICT RISK: AAL101 is taxiing via Alpha to Charlie, which leads to the holding pad at the runway 09 threshold. EJA202 has landed on runway 27 and is still occupying the runway while decelerating toward Bravo midfield. AAL101 must hold short of runway 09 at the Charlie intersection until EJA202 has fully vacated the runway at Bravo and ATC confirms runway clear. If AAL101 continues to the holding pad before EJA202 clears, they will be at the runway threshold while the runway is still occupied — a runway occupancy precursor.
2. CHARLIE SPUR PROXIMITY: Taxiway Charlie leads directly to the runway 09 threshold holding pad. If ATC issues 'line up and wait' or takeoff clearance to AAL101 before EJA202 fully vacates at Bravo, there is a simultaneous occupancy hazard. EJA202 exiting at midfield via Bravo is the slower/later element — ATC must confirm EJA202 clear before issuing any AAL101 runway entry clearance.
required_safety_constraints: Runway is occupied until the landing aircraft has fully vacated the movement area (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-10-3)
ATC must not issue LUAW or takeoff clearance to AAL101 until EJA202 has confirmed clear of runway at Bravo (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-9-4)
AAL101 must hold short of runway 09 at the holding position regardless of prior clearance routing
Runway occupancy time must be confirmed clear before any departure movement onto the runway surface
acceptable_variants: Noting that radio communication with EJA202 to confirm clear is required before AAL101 enters runway is an acceptable additional safety action
evidence_requirements: FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-10-3 — Runway occupancy confirmation
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-9-4 — LUAW and departure clearance timing relative to landing traffic

provenance.generation_rule: KXYZ is a fictional single-runway airport. Taxiway layout (Alpha parallel, Bravo midfield cross, Charlie threshold spur) is a simplified synthetic graph. Simultaneous runway occupancy scenario constructed to probe taxiway graph reasoning. AAL101 and EJA202 are fictional flight numbers using real airline ICAO codes for realism.

prompt:
Airport: Fictional KABC. Layout: Two parallel runways 18L/18R (north-south). Taxiway Alpha runs between the two runways. Taxiway Bravo runs east of runway 18R. Taxiway Delta runs west of runway 18L. Crosswind taxiways Echo (north) and Foxtrot (south) connect Alpha, Bravo, and Delta.

Situation:
- Aircraft UAL500 (B737) is cleared: 'UAL500, runway 18R, cleared for takeoff.'
- Aircraft SWA600 (B737) is cleared: 'SWA600, cross runway 18L, taxi via Alpha, hold short of runway 18R at Alpha.'
- Aircraft DAL700 (A320) is on a 3-mile final for runway 18L.

Current time: 14:23 UTC. Weather: 10SM, CLR, wind 180/10.

Identify all surface movement hazards.
gold_decision:
Three hazards identified:
1. POTENTIAL RUNWAY 18R CONFLICT: UAL500 is cleared for takeoff on runway 18R. SWA600 is cleared to hold short of runway 18R at Alpha. If SWA600 misreads the crossing clearance as a runway 18R crossing (rather than a hold-short), SWA600 could enter runway 18R directly in front of UAL500's takeoff roll. The hold-short instruction 'at Alpha' is on taxiway Alpha between the two runways — this position must be confirmed as short of runway 18R holding position markings.
2. RUNWAY 18L LANDING CONFLICT: DAL700 is on a 3-mile final for runway 18L. SWA600 is crossing runway 18L (explicit crossing clearance given). If SWA600 is slow to cross or stops on runway 18L, DAL700 will arrive while 18L is occupied. ATC must monitor SWA600's crossing speed and runway 18L clearance before DAL700 reaches 1-mile final.
3. SIMULTANEOUS RUNWAY EXPOSURE: UAL500 on takeoff roll on 18R and SWA600 crossing 18L creates simultaneous movement on both parallel runways. If SWA600 overshoots the hold-short on Alpha and enters 18R, the result is a Category A conflict with UAL500.
required_safety_constraints: SWA600's 18L crossing must complete before DAL700 is within 2 miles of the runway threshold (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-10-3)
SWA600 must stop at the runway 18R Alpha holding position — the hold-short is mandatory and must not be confused with a continuation clearance (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2)
ATC must confirm SWA600 clear of runway 18L before DAL700 crosses the 2-mile final mark
Parallel runway operations require ATC to actively manage both runways for simultaneous conflicts
evidence_requirements: FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-10-3 — Runway occupancy and landing traffic separation
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2 — Hold-short mandatory compliance at named position

provenance.generation_rule: KABC is a fictional parallel-runway airport. Taxiway layout between parallel runways (Alpha between 18L/18R, Bravo/Delta outboard) is a standard US parallel runway configuration. Three simultaneous traffic elements (departure, crossing, arrival) create a compound hazard scenario. UAL/SWA/DAL are real airline ICAO codes for realism. All runway designators are fictional.

prompt:
Airport: Fictional KQRS. Layout: Single runway 10/28. Taxiway Alpha parallels runway (south side). Taxiway Bravo: threshold 10, connects Alpha to the runway 10 holding pad. Taxiway Charlie: exits runway midfield to the south. The airport has one active approach: ILS runway 10.

Situation:
- Aircraft SKW881 is on an ILS approach to runway 10, 5 miles out in IMC (ceiling 300ft, visibility 1/4SM, RVR 2400ft).
- Ground vehicle (fuel truck FT-1) has been cleared: 'FT-1, cross runway 10 at Charlie, proceed to south apron.'
- Aircraft ATN502 is holding short of runway 28 on taxiway Alpha awaiting departure clearance.

Identify all surface movement hazards.
gold_decision:
Three critical hazards identified:
1. ILS CRITICAL AREA VIOLATION RISK: SKW881 is on an ILS approach in IMC (ceiling 300ft, RVR 2400ft). Ground vehicle FT-1 has been cleared to cross runway 10 at Charlie. Depending on Charlie's position, FT-1 crossing the runway may be in the ILS critical area for runway 10. In IMC with an active ILS approach, the ILS critical area must be protected — all vehicles and aircraft must hold short of ILS critical area boundaries until the approaching aircraft has landed and cleared. ATC issuing a runway crossing clearance to FT-1 during an active ILS approach in IMC may violate FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-10-4.
2. RUNWAY OCCUPANCY CONFLICT: If FT-1 crosses at Charlie (midfield) while SKW881 is on a 5-mile ILS final, FT-1 must complete the crossing and be clear of the runway before SKW881 reaches the runway threshold. With RVR 2400ft, SKW881 will be at decision altitude (~200ft AGL) in approximately 90-120 seconds. FT-1 must have crossed and cleared the entire runway width before SKW881 commits to land.
3. DEPARTURE INTERFERENCE: ATN502 holding short of runway 28 at Alpha is at the opposite threshold from runway 10. If ATC plans to sequence ATN502 for departure on runway 28 after SKW881 lands, runway occupancy clearance timing must account for SKW881 vacating via Charlie or another taxiway before ATN502 can enter the runway 28 threshold.
required_safety_constraints: ILS critical area must be protected during IMC approaches (ceiling below 800ft or visibility below 2SM) — no vehicle or aircraft may enter critical area during active approach (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-10-4)
FT-1 runway crossing during active ILS approach in IMC is a potential ILS critical area violation requiring immediate assessment
Runway must be confirmed clear before SKW881 crosses decision height — FT-1 must be completely clear of runway 10 before SKW881 is at 1-mile final in RVR 2400ft conditions
Part 121 ILS approach in RVR 2400ft requires all runway and ILS protection areas to be clear
acceptable_variants: FT-1 crossing at Charlie may or may not be in the ILS critical area depending on specific antenna placement — ATC must verify before issuing crossing clearance
evidence_requirements: FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-10-4 — ILS critical area protection
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-10-3 — Runway occupancy during approach
FAA AC 91-73B §4.3 — Low-visibility operations (ILS critical area)

provenance.generation_rule: KQRS is a fictional single-runway airport with ILS approach to runway 10. ILS critical area violation risk combined with runway occupancy conflict during IMC approach. SKW/ATN are real ICAO airline codes. Ground vehicle FT-1 creates the primary ILS critical area conflict.

prompt:
Airport: Fictional KLMN. Layout: Intersecting runways 14/32 and 05/23 (forming an X). Taxiway Alpha: north of runway 05/23 threshold. Taxiway Bravo: south of runway 14 threshold. Taxiway Charlie: southeast, connects to ramp.

Situation:
- Aircraft FFT100 is departing on runway 14 (cleared for takeoff, on takeoff roll, passing taxiway intersection midfield).
- Aircraft JBU200 has been cleared: 'JBU200, cross runway 05, taxi via Alpha, hold short of runway 14.'
- Aircraft SkyTaxi1 (Cessna 172, VFR) has been cleared: 'SkyTaxi1, taxi to runway 23 via Bravo, Charlie, hold short of runway 23.'

Identify all surface movement hazards.
gold_decision:
Three hazards identified:
1. RUNWAY 14 DEPARTURE CONFLICT: FFT100 is on takeoff roll on runway 14. JBU200 is cleared to hold short of runway 14 at taxiway Alpha after crossing runway 05. JBU200 must reach and hold at the runway 14 holding position before FFT100 passes Alpha. If JBU200 does not stop correctly at the hold-short position, JBU200 could enter runway 14 during FFT100's takeoff roll — a Category A conflict.
2. INTERSECTING RUNWAY CONFLICT AT 14/05 INTERSECTION: FFT100's takeoff roll on runway 14 crosses the runway 05/23 surface at the runway intersection. JBU200 is crossing runway 05 at this time. ATC must confirm JBU200 has cleared runway 05 before FFT100's takeoff roll reaches the 05/23 intersection point — or ATC must sequence these events to prevent simultaneous runway 05 and runway 14 occupancy at the intersection.
3. SkyTaxi1 ROUTE VIA BRAVO/CHARLIE: SkyTaxi1's routing via Bravo and Charlie must be verified against the intersecting runway layout. Bravo at the runway 14 threshold could cross runway 14. If Bravo crosses runway 14 before Charlie, SkyTaxi1 needs a crossing clearance for runway 14 — not just a hold-short of runway 23.
required_safety_constraints: Intersecting runway departure requires runway 05/23 to be clear before FFT100's roll reaches the intersection (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-9-7)
JBU200 must stop at runway 14 holding position before FFT100's departure roll reaches that point
SkyTaxi1's taxi route via Bravo must be checked for intermediate runway 14 crossing requirement (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2)
Intersecting runway operations create compound surface conflict potential requiring careful ATC sequencing
evidence_requirements: FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-9-7 — Intersecting runway departure procedures
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2 — Runway crossing authorization

provenance.generation_rule: KLMN is a fictional airport with two intersecting runways (X configuration — a common general aviation airport layout). Intersecting runway conflict during departure from 14 vs crossing runway 05 creates the primary hazard. SkyTaxi1 route analysis tests taxiway graph reasoning for intermediate runway crossings. FFT/JBU are real ICAO codes.

prompt:
Airport: Fictional KPQR. Layout: Single runway 18/36. Taxiway Alpha parallels runway (west). Taxiway Bravo parallels runway (east). Taxiway Charlie (north cross-taxiway) connects Alpha, runway 18 threshold, and Bravo. Taxiway Delta (south cross-taxiway) connects Alpha, runway 36 threshold, and Bravo.

Situation:
- Weather: RVR 600ft, ceiling 100ft OVC, fog. Low visibility operations in effect. SMGCS stop bars installed and active.
- Aircraft GLO301 is cleared: 'GLO301, taxi to runway 18 via Alpha, Charlie.' Stop bars are lit at the runway 18 threshold on Charlie.
- Aircraft ACX402 (holding short runway 18 on Bravo, north side) has stop bars lit in front of it.
- Control tower is coordinating a Cat III ILS approach for CMB501 on 3-mile final.

Identify all surface movement hazards in this LVO scenario.
gold_decision:
Three LVO hazards identified:
1. MANDATORY LIT STOP BAR: GLO301 is taxiing via Alpha to Charlie. At the runway 18 threshold on Charlie, the SMGCS stop bar is LIT. A lit stop bar in SMGCS LVO operations is a MANDATORY STOP — GLO301 must not proceed past the stop bar under any circumstances until ATC extinguishes it and issues explicit clearance. A lit stop bar overrun is a mandatory stop bar violation and potential runway incursion in RVR 600ft conditions (FAA AC 91-73B §4.3, FAA AC 120-57B).
2. SIMULTANEOUS ENTRY RISK: Both GLO301 (via Charlie, north) and ACX402 (holding on Bravo, north) are positioned at or near the runway 18 entry points. If ATC extinguishes the stop bar for one aircraft and the other also proceeds, two aircraft could simultaneously enter runway 18. ATC must extinguish only one stop bar at a time and must not issue dual runway entry in LVO.
3. ILS CRITICAL AREA PROTECTION FOR CMB501: CMB501 is on a Cat III ILS approach in RVR 600ft. The ILS critical area for runway 18 must be fully protected. No ground vehicle or aircraft may be in the critical area. GLO301's position at the Charlie hold and ACX402's position on Bravo must both be verified as outside the ILS critical area boundary before ATC proceeds with the approach.
required_safety_constraints: Lit SMGCS stop bars are mandatory stop points — no aircraft or vehicle may cross a lit stop bar (FAA AC 120-57B §4.3)
Cat III ILS critical area protection is absolute in RVR 600ft — all surface traffic must be verified outside critical area (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-10-4)
ATC must issue only one stop bar clearance at a time in LVO — dual runway entry clearances are prohibited in SMGCS operations
Stop bar clearance (extinguishing the bar) must be accompanied by explicit verbal ATC clearance for runway entry (FAA AC 120-57B)
evidence_requirements: FAA AC 120-57B — SMGCS stop bar operations
FAA AC 91-73B §4.3 — LVO pilot surface movement
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-10-4 — Cat III ILS critical area protection

provenance.generation_rule: KPQR is a fictional single-runway airport with SMGCS LVO infrastructure. RVR 600ft / Cat III ILS approach creates LVO scenario. SMGCS stop bar mandatory stop rule from FAA AC 120-57B. ILS critical area Cat III protection from FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-10-4. GLO/ACX/CMB are real airline ICAO codes.

prompt:
Airport: Fictional KUVW. Layout: Runway 10/28 (east-west). Taxiway Alpha parallels runway (south). Taxiway Bravo exits runway 10 threshold to the south. Taxiway Charlie (midfield exit south) connects runway to Alpha midfield. Hot spot HS-1 documented at Alpha/Charlie intersection: 'reduced sight lines to runway 28 threshold due to terminal building.'

Situation:
- Aircraft MXY701 (B737-800, landing) has been cleared to land runway 10 and will exit at Charlie.
- Aircraft QXE802 (Dash 8, departure) is cleared: 'QXE802, taxi to runway 28 via Alpha. At Charlie, hold short of runway 10.'
- Current time: 08:15 local. Weather: 5SM, SCT015, wind 095/12.

Identify all surface movement hazards.
gold_decision:
Three hazards identified:
1. HOT SPOT HS-1 SIGHT LINE RISK: QXE802 is instructed to hold short of runway 10 at Charlie (the HS-1 intersection). HS-1 has documented poor sight lines to the runway 28 threshold. QXE802's crew, approaching Charlie from the west on Alpha, will have difficulty seeing traffic on runway 10 due to the terminal building obstruction. QXE802 must slow significantly before reaching the Charlie intersection to ensure they stop at the hold-short position without overshooting onto runway 10.
2. RUNWAY EXIT CONFLICT: MXY701 is landing on runway 10 and will exit at Charlie — the same intersection where QXE802 is directed to hold short. MXY701 exiting the runway at Charlie while QXE802 is stopped at the Charlie/Alpha hold-short creates a converging traffic situation. ATC must ensure MXY701 has fully exited the runway and is clear of the runway holding position at Charlie before directing QXE802 to proceed past the hold-short.
3. SIGHT LINE/HOLD-SHORT OVERSHOOT RISK: Given HS-1's documented poor sight lines, QXE802 approaching the Charlie hold-short may not see the runway holding position marking until very close (compounded by the 5SM visibility and scattered clouds reducing visual clarity). If QXE802 overshoots the hold-short, QXE802 could conflict with MXY701 vacating at Charlie.
required_safety_constraints: HS-1 requires reduced taxi speed and heightened vigilance as documented in airport diagram legend (FAA AC 91-73B §3.5)
QXE802 must stop completely at the runway 10 hold-short at Charlie — overshoot into runway 10 is a runway incursion
ATC must confirm MXY701 clear of Charlie/runway 10 junction before any QXE802 runway 10 crossing or continued taxi
Hot spot sight line limitation must be communicated in ATIS or pilot advisory if operationally significant
evidence_requirements: FAA AC 91-73B §3.5 — Hot spot operational procedures
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-10-3 — Runway occupancy and exit confirmation

provenance.generation_rule: KUVW is a fictional airport. Hot spot HS-1 sight line scenario is representative of documented US airport hot spots where terminal buildings create sight-line obstructions. MXY/QXE are real ICAO airline codes. Converging traffic at a hot spot intersection is a compound hazard.

prompt:
Airport: Fictional KEFG. Layout: Parallel runways 27L/27R (east-west). Between the runways: Taxiway Alpha (east-west central taxiway). North of 27L: Taxiway Bravo. South of 27R: Taxiway Charlie. North cross-taxiways: Delta (west end), Echo (midfield), Foxtrot (east end). South cross-taxiways: Golf (west), Hotel (midfield), India (east).

Situation (time 17:45Z, night VMC, wind 270/14):
- AAL111: cleared for takeoff runway 27L
- UAL222: cleared 'cross runway 27L at Echo, hold short of runway 27R at Alpha'
- SWA333: on 3-mile final runway 27R
- DAL444: cleared 'line up and wait runway 27R'

Identify all hazards.
gold_decision:
Four hazards identified:
1. UAL222 vs AAL111 CONFLICT: UAL222 is cleared to cross runway 27L at Echo. AAL111 is cleared for takeoff on runway 27L. If AAL111 begins its takeoff roll, UAL222 must have already cleared runway 27L at Echo before AAL111 reaches the Echo intersection. ATC must confirm UAL222 has crossed and is clear of runway 27L before AAL111's roll begins or reaches Echo.
2. LUAW vs SWA333 LANDING CONFLICT: DAL444 is instructed to line up and wait on runway 27R. SWA333 is on a 3-mile final for runway 27R. Per FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-9-4, LUAW is prohibited when an arriving aircraft is within 2 miles of the threshold. At 3 miles and closing, DAL444's LUAW may be permissible now but ATC must monitor — if SWA333 closes to 2 miles before ATC issues takeoff clearance to DAL444, SWA333 must go-around or DAL444 must be cleared for takeoff immediately.
3. UAL222 HOLD-SHORT AT ALPHA: After crossing 27L at Echo, UAL222 must stop at the runway 27R holding position on Alpha. With DAL444 in LUAW on 27R and SWA333 on 3-mile final, Alpha between the runways is occupied by UAL222. If UAL222 overshoots the 27R hold-short on Alpha and enters runway 27R, DAL444 (in LUAW) and SWA333 (approaching) are endangered.
4. NIGHT OPERATIONS COMPLEXITY: Night VMC conditions reduce visual situational awareness for all crews. AAL111's departure lights, UAL222's position at Echo, and DAL444 in LUAW on 27R are all concurrent night-movement elements requiring enhanced ATC sequencing.
required_safety_constraints: LUAW within 2 miles of arriving traffic is prohibited (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-9-4(b))
UAL222's runway 27L crossing must complete before AAL111 begins takeoff roll at Echo (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2)
UAL222 must stop at runway 27R hold-short on Alpha — failure creates conflict with DAL444 in LUAW and SWA333 final
Night operations require enhanced ATC traffic call-outs to LUAW aircraft per FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-9-4 night restrictions
evidence_requirements: FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-9-4 — LUAW night restriction and 2-mile rule
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2 — Runway crossing timing relative to departures

provenance.generation_rule: KEFG is a fictional parallel runway airport at night. Four simultaneous traffic elements create compound parallel-runway conflict scenario. LUAW 2-mile rule per FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-9-4(b). Night LUAW restriction per §3-9-4(c). Crossing conflict timing from §3-7-2. UAL/AAL/SWA/DAL are real ICAO codes.

prompt:
Airport: Fictional KHIJ. Layout: Single runway 14/32. Taxiway Alpha (southeast parallel). Taxiway Bravo: runway 14 threshold (east). Taxiway Charlie: exits runway midfield southeast. Taxiway Delta: runway 32 threshold (northwest). ILS installed for runway 14. Hot spot HS-2 documented at Alpha/Bravo intersection: 'confusion between taxiway Bravo and runway 14 entry.'

Situation:
- Weather: 10SM CLR, wind 140/12.
- Aircraft COM901 (regional, departure runway 14) is cleared: 'COM901, taxi to runway 14 via Alpha, Bravo.'
- COM901 crew is new to KHIJ. First Officer is flying the taxi leg.
- No other traffic on the ground or in the air.

Identify all surface movement hazards.
gold_decision:
Two hazards identified:
1. HOT SPOT HS-2 WRONG ENTRY RISK: Hot spot HS-2 documents confusion between taxiway Bravo and runway 14 entry at the Alpha/Bravo intersection. COM901 is taxiing via Alpha to Bravo to reach runway 14. At this intersection, the taxiway Bravo entry and the runway 14 entry are confusingly similar in appearance. The First Officer (new to KHIJ) may confuse taxiway Bravo leading to the runway threshold with the runway 14 entry itself, potentially entering the runway at the incorrect point or without pausing at the holding position.
2. CREW SITUATIONAL AWARENESS RISK: COM901 crew is new to KHIJ with a First Officer flying the taxi. 'New to airport' is a documented runway incursion risk factor (ASRS report analysis). The crew must brief the HS-2 hot spot, confirm taxi route on the airport diagram before taxi, and slow at the Alpha/Bravo intersection to positively identify the runway 14 holding position marking before entering.
required_safety_constraints: HS-2 requires pre-taxi briefing and reduced speed at the Alpha/Bravo intersection (FAA AC 91-73B §3.5)
New-to-airport crew must positively verify position on airport diagram at the HS-2 intersection before proceeding
Runway 14 entry must be at the correct holding position marking — any ambiguity about which pavement surface is the runway requires a stop and ATC query
Even with no conflicting traffic, wrong runway entry is a runway incursion event regardless of collision risk (FAA Order 7050.1B Category D)
acceptable_variants: Captain should take taxi leg controls at a hot spot if First Officer unfamiliar with airport — crew coordination responsibility
evidence_requirements: FAA AC 91-73B §3.5 — Hot spot procedures and new-to-airport risk factor
FAA Order 7050.1B — Runway incursion even with no conflicting traffic (Category D definition)

provenance.generation_rule: KHIJ is a fictional airport with a single runway and documented taxiway/runway confusion hot spot. HS-2 'confusion between taxiway and runway entry' is a realistic hot spot type at US regional airports. New-to-airport crew risk factor documented in ASRS report statistics. COM901 is a fictional flight number.

prompt:
Airport: Fictional KSTU. Layout: Runway 09/27 (east-west). Taxiway Alpha (north parallel). Taxiway Bravo (south parallel). Cross-taxiways: Charlie (west end), Delta (midfield), Echo (east end). Runway 27 displaced threshold (300ft from runway end).

Situation:
- Aircraft GTI001 (B747-400F, heavy) has landed runway 09 and is decelerating, passing midfield (taxiway Delta intersection).
- Aircraft FDX002 (B777F, heavy) is cleared: 'FDX002, cross runway 09 at Delta, hold short of runway 27.'
- Aircraft GROUND VEHICLE GV-3 is cleared: 'GV-3, runway 09 at Echo, cleared to cross.'

Identify all surface movement hazards.
gold_decision:
Three hazards identified:
1. RUNWAY OCCUPANCY — GTI001 vs FDX002: GTI001 is still on runway 09, decelerating past Delta. FDX002 has been cleared to cross runway 09 at Delta. These two events are in direct conflict: FDX002 entering runway 09 at Delta while GTI001 is passing through the Delta intersection creates a collision risk. ATC must not have issued the FDX002 crossing clearance while GTI001 is still on the runway at or approaching Delta. FDX002 must hold short of runway 09 at Delta until GTI001 has passed Delta and is clear.
2. RUNWAY OCCUPANCY — GTI001 vs GV-3: GTI001 is decelerating on runway 09 and will eventually reach Echo (east end). GV-3 is cleared to cross runway 09 at Echo. If GTI001 takes longer to decelerate than expected (B747 on a long landing roll), GTI001 may reach Echo before GV-3 has cleared the runway. ATC must confirm Echo is clear of GTI001 before GV-3 crosses at Echo.
3. DISPLACED THRESHOLD vs RUNWAY 27 HOLD: FDX002 is cleared to hold short of runway 27 after crossing runway 09. The runway 27 displaced threshold means the runway 27 holding position marking may be at the full runway end, not the displaced threshold — FDX002 must hold short of the runway 27 full threshold (beyond the displaced threshold zone) as directed.
required_safety_constraints: Landing aircraft (GTI001) has priority — runway surface must not be accessed by any crossing traffic until GTI001 has cleared past the crossing point (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-10-3)
Heavy aircraft (B747) have longer landing roll distances — account for extended runway occupancy time when issuing crossing clearances
GV-3 crossing clearance at Echo must be timed after GTI001 clears that intersection
Displaced threshold does not move the runway holding position — FDX002 must hold short of the full runway 27 threshold marking
evidence_requirements: FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-10-3 — Runway occupancy during landing roll
FAA AC 150/5340-1L §2.11 — Displaced threshold markings and holding position implications

provenance.generation_rule: KSTU is a fictional cargo-oriented airport (heavy aircraft). B747/B777 heavy landing roll on a single runway creates timing-sensitive crossing conflicts. Displaced threshold adds a knowledge component about holding positions. GTI/FDX are real ICAO cargo carrier codes.

prompt:
Airport: Fictional KNOP. Layout: Runway 03/21 (north-south angled). Taxiway Alpha runs parallel to runway west side. Taxiway Bravo: southern end, connects Alpha to runway 21 threshold. Hot spot HS-1: at Alpha/Bravo junction, documented 'confusing runway 21 holding position sign due to sign occlusion by foliage.'

Situation:
- NOTAM active: 'KNOTAM A0123: HS-1 RUNWAY HOLDING POSITION SIGN RWY 21 PARTIALLY OBSCURED BY VEGETATION. PILOTS EXERCISE CAUTION.'
- Aircraft PSA501 is cleared: 'PSA501, taxi to runway 21 via Alpha, Bravo.'
- Weather: Dusk, 8SM, FEW020, wind calm.

Identify all surface movement hazards.
gold_decision:
Two hazards identified:
1. OBSCURED MANDATORY SIGN AT HS-1: The NOTAM specifically warns that the runway 21 holding position sign (mandatory red/white sign) at Alpha/Bravo is partially obscured by vegetation. This is the primary hazard: PSA501's crew may not see or clearly read the mandatory hold-short sign as they approach runway 21 via Alpha/Bravo. Without clear sign identification, the crew may not recognize the hold-short point and may overshoot the runway holding position marking and enter runway 21 without clearance.
2. DUSK LIGHTING DEGRADATION: Dusk conditions reduce visual contrast for pavement markings. The two-solid/two-dashed runway holding position markings at Bravo/runway 21 will be less visible in transitional lighting compared to full daylight. Combined with the obscured sign at HS-1, PSA501 must slow significantly before reaching the Bravo intersection and positively identify the pavement markings on the taxiway surface rather than relying on the sign.
required_safety_constraints: Obscured mandatory instruction sign requires heightened reliance on pavement markings for hold-short position identification (FAA AC 150/5340-1L §2.17)
PSA501 crew must be briefed on HS-1 NOTAM before taxi and must slow at the Bravo intersection (FAA AC 91-73B §3.5)
If the holding position cannot be positively identified, PSA501 must stop before any runway surface is reached and contact ATC
Airport operator is responsible for maintaining unobstructed mandatory signs — a NOTAM does not waive the obstruction, it only warns of it
evidence_requirements: FAA AC 91-73B §3.5 — Hot spot and NOTAM-flagged hazard operational procedures
FAA AC 150/5340-1L §2.4 — Mandatory instruction sign maintenance requirements

provenance.generation_rule: KNOP is a fictional airport with a documented sign-obscuration hot spot per a synthetic NOTAM. Vegetation-obscured mandatory signs are a realistic airport safety issue. Dusk conditions compound the visual hazard. PSA is a real ICAO airline code. Scenario tests NOTAM-hazard integration with airport diagram situational awareness.

prompt:
Airport: Fictional KRST. Layout: Runway 08/26. Taxiway Alpha (south parallel). Cross-taxiways Bravo (west), Charlie (midfield), Delta (east). Taxiway Echo: northern bypass taxiway from Bravo to Delta, used as a holding area.

Situation (high traffic, 16:30 local, summer):
- Aircraft HAV601 cleared for takeoff runway 08.
- Aircraft WAJ702 cleared 'line up and wait runway 08' — is behind HAV601, waiting on taxiway Bravo at the holding position.
- Aircraft STX803 (arriving, 1.8 miles final for runway 26 — opposite direction to HAV601 departure).

Identify all surface movement hazards.
gold_decision:
Three critical hazards:
1. LUAW WITH ARRIVING TRAFFIC WITHIN 2 MILES: WAJ702 is issued LUAW on runway 08. STX803 is on a 1.8-mile final for runway 26 (opposite direction). FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-9-4(b) prohibits LUAW on a runway when an arriving aircraft is within 2 miles of the threshold — and opposite-direction arrivals share the same runway. STX803 at 1.8 miles is BELOW the 2-mile threshold, meaning the WAJ702 LUAW clearance may have been improperly issued if STX803 had crossed 2 miles before the clearance was issued.
2. OPPOSITE DIRECTION CONFLICT: HAV601 is departing runway 08 (heading east). STX803 is arriving runway 26 (also heading east — landing on the same runway opposite end). If HAV601's departure is delayed or if HAV601 does not clear the runway before STX803 crosses a critical point, the two aircraft are on a direct head-on conflict on the same runway.
3. RUNWAY 08 TRIPLE OCCUPANCY RISK: HAV601 (departing), WAJ702 (LUAW), and STX803 (arriving) are all associated with runway 08/26 simultaneously. This is a high-risk compound scenario requiring ATC to actively manage departure completion, LUAW status, and arrival timing in real time.
required_safety_constraints: LUAW is prohibited when arriving aircraft is within 2 miles of threshold (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-9-4(b)) — STX803 at 1.8 miles may violate this rule
Opposite-direction operations on same runway require strict separation between departure clearance and arriving aircraft position
WAJ702 in LUAW must be immediately cleared for takeoff or instructed to exit the runway if STX803 cannot be managed safely
Controller must issue go-around to STX803 if runway cannot be confirmed clear before STX803 crosses the runway threshold
evidence_requirements: FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-9-4(b) — LUAW 2-mile restriction
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-9-7 — Opposite direction operations

provenance.generation_rule: KRST is a fictional single-runway airport. LUAW 2-mile prohibition from FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-9-4(b). Opposite-direction operations from §3-9-7. Three simultaneous runway-associated aircraft creates a compound critical scenario. HAV/WAJ/STX are fictional flight prefixes for distinctness.

prompt:
Airport: Fictional KMVW. Layout: Runway 17/35. Taxiway Alpha east parallel. Taxiway Bravo: runway 17 (south) threshold entry. Taxiway Charlie: midfield east exit. Taxiway Delta: runway 35 (north) threshold entry.

Situation:
- Night operations, VMC, wind 170/08.
- Aircraft BEE901 has received: 'BEE901, taxi to runway 35 via Alpha, Delta.' This routing takes BEE901 north on Alpha, then west on Delta to the runway 35 threshold.
- Along taxiway Alpha northbound, there is an unmarked construction zone between Charlie and Delta. A NOTAM is active: 'A0234: TAXIWAY ALPHA BETWEEN CHARLIE AND DELTA CLSD — CONSTRUCTION EQUIPMENT PRESENT.'
- BEE901 crew has not received this NOTAM. ATC did not include it in the taxi clearance.

Identify all hazards.
gold_decision:
Three hazards:
1. ACTIVE CONSTRUCTION ON CLEARED TAXI ROUTE: BEE901 is taxiing via Alpha, which has an active construction closure between Charlie and Delta. The NOTAM prohibits use of this taxiway segment. ATC issued a taxi clearance via Alpha without re-routing around the construction — this is an ATC error. BEE901 will encounter construction equipment in darkness with no warning if the route is not corrected.
2. CREW UNAWARE OF NOTAM: BEE901 crew did not receive the NOTAM. The taxi clearance does not mention the construction. Crew has no information to suspect the route is blocked or hazardous. At night, the construction zone may not be visible until BEE901 is adjacent to the equipment — by which point there may be insufficient space to stop or maneuver.
3. COLLISION WITH CONSTRUCTION EQUIPMENT: If BEE901 proceeds as cleared via Alpha through the Charlie-Delta segment, BEE901 could encounter construction equipment, personnel, or barriers in darkness, risking foreign object damage, propeller/engine strike, or aircraft/personnel injury.
required_safety_constraints: ATC must not issue taxi clearance via a closed taxiway segment — controller must know active NOTAMs and re-route accordingly (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-1)
Crew must check all active NOTAMs before taxi — a closed taxiway NOTAM is a mandatory operational restriction
If crew discovers a discrepancy between their NOTAM knowledge and the taxi route, they must query ATC before proceeding into an uncertain area
Night conditions make construction equipment on taxiways a FOD and collision hazard — reduced visibility at night significantly elevates this risk
evidence_requirements: FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-1 — Taxi route issuance and NOTAM awareness
FAA AC 91-73B §3.4 — Pre-taxi NOTAM review requirement

provenance.generation_rule: KMVW is a fictional airport. Construction closure NOTAM on the taxi route is a realistic surface hazard category. ATC error (issuing route via closed taxiway) and crew NOTAM miss combine as compound failure modes. Night conditions elevate physical hazard risk. BEE is a real ICAO airline code.

prompt:
Airport: Fictional KZAB. Layout: Parallel runways 09L/09R. Taxiway Alpha between the runways. Taxiway Bravo: north of 09L. Taxiway Charlie: south of 09R. Cross-taxiways Delta (west) and Echo (east).

Situation:
- Aircraft AAL001 cleared 'cross runway 09L at Delta, cross runway 09R at Delta, taxi to terminal via Charlie.'
- This clearance contains two runway crossings in a single instruction.
- No other traffic.

Identify the hazard in this clearance.
gold_decision: The clearance contains two runway crossings issued simultaneously — this is a procedural violation of FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2(b), which states controllers must issue runway crossing clearances one at a time. The hazard is that if AAL001 encounters any unexpected traffic, conflict, or navigation confusion on the first runway crossing, the crew may assume they have clearance to proceed through the second runway without stopping or waiting for explicit re-authorization. The dual-crossing clearance structure removes the safety check that occurs when a crew must stop, receive clearance, and confirm each crossing individually. This is also a known runway incursion precursor pattern documented in FAA Runway Safety Program analysis.
required_safety_constraints: Controllers must issue runway crossing clearances one at a time (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2(b))
A dual-crossing clearance is non-standard and should not be read back without querying ATC about the second crossing
AAL001 should hold short of runway 09L at Delta, receive clearance, cross, stop at 09R at Delta, and await a second crossing clearance
If AAL001 receives a dual-crossing clearance, the crew should confirm with ATC that both crossings are authorized simultaneously or request separate clearances
evidence_requirements: FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2(b) — One-at-a-time runway crossing clearance rule

provenance.generation_rule: KZAB is a fictional parallel-runway airport. Dual runway crossing in a single clearance violates FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2(b). This scenario is a direct test of the one-at-a-time crossing rule. AAL is a real ICAO code.

prompt:
Airport: Fictional KPOP. Layout: Single runway 06/24. Taxiway Alpha (south parallel). Taxiway Bravo (threshold 06 south). Taxiway Charlie (midfield south exit). Taxiway Delta (threshold 24 south).

Situation:
- Aircraft ERJ001 has been cleared to land runway 24. Weather: 6SM, OVC010, wind 240/14.
- Aircraft ERJ001 is on final, 1 mile out.
- Aircraft TRP002 has been cleared: 'TRP002, runway 24, cleared for takeoff.'
- TRP002 is beginning its takeoff roll on runway 24, heading 240.
- ERJ001 is 1 mile final, also heading 240 (same runway, same direction).

Identify all hazards.
gold_decision: One catastrophic hazard: SIMULTANEOUS SAME-DIRECTION RUNWAY OCCUPANCY CONFLICT. TRP002 is taking off on runway 24 heading 240 while ERJ001 is on a 1-mile final for the same runway heading 240. Both aircraft are on the same runway in the same direction. TRP002 is accelerating on the ground while ERJ001 is 1 mile away and closing at approach speed. Unless TRP002 becomes airborne and climbs away from the runway environment before ERJ001 crosses the threshold, the two aircraft will be in simultaneous runway occupancy — a potential catastrophic collision. ERJ001 must immediately be issued a go-around instruction by ATC. ATC should not have cleared TRP002 for takeoff with ERJ001 on a 1-mile final.
required_safety_constraints: A landing clearance and a simultaneous takeoff clearance on the same runway is an ATC error constituting unsafe separation (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-10-3)
ERJ001 must receive immediate go-around instruction — this is an emergency-equivalent situation requiring priority action
TRP002 must be advised of traffic (ERJ001) on final and told to expedite departure if safe, or reject takeoff
ATC must not clear aircraft for takeoff on a runway where another aircraft is within 1 mile final
evidence_requirements: FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-10-3 — Runway separation standards for simultaneous takeoff/landing

provenance.generation_rule: KPOP is a fictional single-runway airport. Simultaneous same-runway takeoff and landing clearance is a direct Category A runway incursion scenario. ERJ/TRP are real ICAO codes. 1-mile final timing creates an imminently dangerous scenario requiring immediate go-around.

prompt:
Airport: Fictional KBVW. Layout: Single runway 12/30. Taxiway Alpha (southeast parallel). Taxiway Bravo (threshold 12 entry). Taxiway Charlie (midfield exit). ILS runway 12 installed.

Situation (daytime, 10SM, wind 120/10):
- Aircraft CLX801 is on a 4-mile ILS final for runway 12.
- Aircraft FDX802 is holding short of runway 30 at Alpha, awaiting departure clearance.
- Ground vehicle GV-1 is in the ILS critical area for runway 12, performing a runway friction test. ATC has not issued ILS critical area protection for CLX801's approach.

Identify all hazards.
gold_decision:
Two hazards:
1. ILS CRITICAL AREA VIOLATION: GV-1 is in the ILS critical area while CLX801 is on a 4-mile ILS final. The ILS critical area protection requirement (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-10-4) applies whenever an ILS approach is in progress. Although the weather is 10SM (above LVO minimums), the ILS critical area must still be protected if CLX801 is flying an ILS approach in IMC-equivalent procedures or if ATC has not explicitly determined that LVO protection is unnecessary. GV-1's presence in the critical area could cause ILS signal distortion even in VMC conditions if close to the antenna. ATC should have issued critical area hold-short to GV-1 before CLX801 commenced the ILS approach.
2. DEPARTURE TIMING: FDX802 is holding short of runway 30 at Alpha. With CLX801 on a 4-mile ILS final for runway 12, any departure on runway 30 must be timed so FDX802 can depart and climb clear before CLX801 crosses the runway 12 threshold. Opposite-direction departure on runway 30 while CLX801 is on final for runway 12 requires specific separation standards per FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-9-7.
required_safety_constraints: ILS critical area must be protected whenever an ILS approach is in progress — GV-1 must exit the critical area and ATC must issue hold-short (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-10-4)
Opposite-direction departure on runway 30 while runway 12 ILS is in progress requires ATC confirmation of safe separation (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-9-7)
ATC error in not issuing ILS critical area protection for GV-1 must be corrected immediately — GV-1 must exit or hold short
ILS signal distortion from vehicles in critical area is a hazard even in VMC conditions
evidence_requirements: FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-10-4 — ILS critical area protection during active approach
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-9-7 — Opposite direction operations

provenance.generation_rule: KBVW is a fictional airport with ILS runway 12. ILS critical area vehicle scenario tests knowledge that protection applies during active ILS approaches not just LVO. CLX/FDX are real ICAO cargo codes. GV-1 runway friction test is a realistic maintenance scenario.

prompt:
Airport: Fictional KWXZ. Layout: Runway 01/19. Taxiway Alpha (west parallel). Taxiway Bravo (threshold 01 south). Cross-taxiway Charlie (midfield). Runway 19 approach uses PAPI only (no ILS).

Situation (dusk, 3SM, SCT006, wind 190/08):
- Aircraft ARN901 on a visual approach to runway 19, 2 miles out, PAPI indicates 4 white (high).
- Aircraft VRD402 cleared: 'VRD402, cross runway 01 at Charlie, taxi to ramp.'
- Aircraft GEC503 holding short runway 19 on Bravo, cleared: 'GEC503, line up and wait runway 01.'
- Wait — the controller said 'runway 01' for GEC503, which is the opposite end of the same runway where ARN901 is landing runway 19.

Identify all hazards.
gold_decision:
Three hazards:
1. LUAW OPPOSITE DIRECTION TO LANDING TRAFFIC: GEC503 is instructed to 'line up and wait runway 01,' which is the opposite end of runway 19 where ARN901 is on a 2-mile final. FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-9-4 prohibits LUAW when arriving aircraft is within 2 miles of the threshold. ARN901 at 2 miles is at exactly the threshold distance — LUAW clearance to GEC503 is at best marginal and likely prohibited. If ATC intends GEC503 to depart after ARN901 lands, GEC503 should hold short of runway 01 and not be given LUAW until ARN901 has landed and cleared the runway.
2. ARN901 PAPI 4-WHITE (HIGH APPROACH): ARN901's PAPI shows 4 white, indicating the aircraft is significantly above the glidepath. A high approach in 3SM / SCT006 (600ft ceiling) conditions means ARN901 may need to descend aggressively to land within the runway environment. A high approach increases the risk of a long landing, runway exceedance, or unstabilized approach — increasing runway occupancy time.
3. VRD402 RUNWAY CROSSING TIMING: VRD402 is crossing runway 01 at Charlie (midfield) during the same time period that ARN901 is on a 2-mile final. If ARN901's approach is high and the landing is long, ARN901 may decelerate slowly and still be on the runway at Charlie when VRD402 is completing the crossing. ATC must confirm VRD402 will be clear of runway 01/19 before ARN901 can reach the Charlie area.
required_safety_constraints: LUAW prohibited with arriving aircraft within 2 miles of threshold (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-9-4(b))
PAPI 4-white approach indicates high path — must be corrected before landing or a go-around should be executed per standard stabilized approach criteria
VRD402 crossing at Charlie creates a runway occupancy timing conflict with a potentially long-landing ARN901
ATC must sequence GEC503 LUAW and VRD402 crossing to ensure both are resolved before ARN901 arrives over the threshold
evidence_requirements: FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-9-4(b) — LUAW 2-mile restriction
FAA AIM §2-1-2 — PAPI indication interpretation (4 white = high)
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-10-3 — Runway occupancy conflict

provenance.generation_rule: KWXZ is a fictional airport. PAPI 4-white high approach per FAA AIM §2-1-2. LUAW 2-mile rule per FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-9-4(b). Three compound hazards: LUAW opposite direction, PAPI high approach, and midfield crossing. ARN/VRD/GEC are real ICAO airline codes.

prompt:
Airport: Fictional KTYW. Layout: Runway 16/34. Taxiway Alpha east parallel. Taxiway Bravo: threshold 16 entry. Taxiway Charlie: midfield exit. Taxiway Delta: threshold 34 entry.

Situation (daytime, 10SM, wind 160/10):
- Aircraft QBE201 (Cessna 172, student solo) is cleared: 'QBE201, taxi to runway 16, via Alpha, Bravo.'
- Aircraft QBE201's student pilot stops at the runway 16 holding position on Bravo and waits. Tower then says: 'QBE201, runway 16, cleared for takeoff.'
- After clearance, QBE201 enters runway 16 and lines up. However, before beginning the takeoff roll, the student pilot hears: 'Traffic, traffic' on TCAS from another aircraft.
- A second aircraft (RPX302) has just been cleared to land runway 34 (opposite direction) and is on a 1-mile final.

Identify all hazards.
gold_decision:
Two critical hazards:
1. SIMULTANEOUS OPPOSITE-DIRECTION OPERATIONS: QBE201 has been cleared for takeoff on runway 16. RPX302 has been cleared to land on runway 34 — the opposite end of the same runway. These clearances create a head-on conflict on runway 16/34. The aircraft are approaching each other — QBE201 beginning takeoff roll heading south, RPX302 on a 1-mile final heading north, both on the same runway. This is a Category A runway incursion precursor and potential collision scenario. ATC has committed a separation error by clearing both simultaneously.
2. STUDENT PILOT INEXPERIENCE: QBE201's pilot is a student on solo flight. A student pilot encountering a TCAS traffic alert, a runway conflict, and an ATC error simultaneously is at high risk of hesitation, incorrect action, or failure to immediately reject the takeoff and exit the runway. The student pilot must: (a) immediately reject the takeoff, (b) hold position or exit the runway, (c) contact tower immediately. Instructor/tower must issue immediate go-around or abort instruction.
required_safety_constraints: ATC must issue immediate go-around to RPX302 and abort/hold instruction to QBE201 — this is an emergency traffic alert scenario (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-1-8)
Opposite-direction takeoff and landing clearances on same runway is a critical separation failure
Student solo pilot receiving simultaneous traffic warning requires immediate, clear ATC instruction — do not assume student will self-assess correctly
QBE201 must reject the takeoff immediately upon receiving TCAS traffic alert in this scenario — remaining on runway 34/16 while RPX302 is on 1-mile final is unsafe
evidence_requirements: FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-1-8 — Safety alert issuance and emergency traffic instructions
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-9-7 — Opposite direction runway conflict procedures

provenance.generation_rule: KTYW is a fictional GA airport. Student solo pilot compound scenario: opposite-direction runway clearance error plus TCAS alert. ATC separation error is the root cause. Student pilot inexperience is a compounding factor. QBE/RPX are fictional airline-style codes used for distinctness.

prompt:
Airport: Fictional KFGP. Layout: Runway 09/27 east-west. Taxiway Alpha south parallel. Cross-taxiways Bravo (west), Charlie (midfield), Delta (east). Construction zone: South apron area — cones and temporary fencing present, but taxiway Alpha itself is open.

Situation:
- Night operations, VMC, 10SM, wind 090/06.
- Aircraft MHT101 (B737) is taxiing via Alpha eastbound, en route from south apron to runway 09 threshold via Delta.
- A construction crane (nighttime lighting only — top obstruction light) is positioned at the south edge of taxiway Alpha near the Charlie intersection, within the taxiway object-free area, but with no NOTAM and no ATC warning issued.

Identify all hazards.
gold_decision:
Two hazards:
1. UNREPORTED OBSTRUCTION IN TAXIWAY OBJECT-FREE AREA: The construction crane is positioned within the taxiway object-free area (within 89 feet of centerline for a B737 class aircraft per FAA AC 150/5300-13B). A crane in this area without a NOTAM is an uncharted hazard. MHT101 is a B737 with a wide wingspan — at night, the crew may not see the crane (identified only by a top obstruction light) in time to avoid a wingtip strike. The absence of a NOTAM means MHT101's crew has no pre-taxi warning.
2. NO NOTAM FILED: The crane's presence has not been reported to ATC or NOTAM authorities. This is an airport operations compliance failure — any construction equipment within taxiway object-free areas must be NOTAMed (FAA AC 150/5370-2G). ATC issuing a taxi clearance via Alpha is unaware of the crane and has not issued a special caution. MHT101 is proceeding on a validly issued clearance into an unreported hazard.
required_safety_constraints: Objects within taxiway object-free areas require a NOTAM and ATC coordination (FAA AC 150/5370-2G)
Taxiway object-free area (TOFA) for Group IV aircraft (B737) extends 40.5m from centerline per FAA AC 150/5300-13B
Night operations compound the visual detection problem — low-visibility crane encounter at night creates FOD and wingtip strike risk
Airport operator must immediately issue a NOTAM and restrict Alpha if crane is within TOFA
evidence_requirements: FAA AC 150/5370-2G — Construction safety on airport operations areas (NOTAM requirement for objects in movement areas)
FAA AC 150/5300-13B — Airport design, taxiway object-free area dimensions

provenance.generation_rule: KFGP is a fictional airport. Unreported crane in taxiway object-free area during night operations is a realistic airport construction safety scenario. FAA AC 150/5370-2G NOTAM requirement for construction objects. TOFA dimensions per FAA AC 150/5300-13B for Group IV (B737-class) aircraft. MHT is a real ICAO airline code.

prompt:
Airport: Fictional KDMZ. Layout: Single runway 05/23. Taxiway Alpha (southeast parallel). Taxiway Bravo (threshold 05 entry). Taxiway Charlie (midfield exit). Taxiway Delta (threshold 23 entry).

Situation (daytime, 10SM, wind 050/14):
- Aircraft TGO501 cleared 'line up and wait runway 05.'
- TGO501 has been on LUAW for 3 minutes 45 seconds with no takeoff clearance issued.
- Aircraft JAL602 is on a 6-mile final for runway 05.
- ATC is busy with other frequency — no communication with TGO501 for 4 minutes.

Identify all hazards.
gold_decision:
Three hazards:
1. EXCESSIVE LUAW HOLD TIME: TGO501 has been in LUAW for nearly 4 minutes with no takeoff clearance and no ATC communication. FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-9-4 requires the controller to advise the LUAW aircraft of any reason for delay and to cancel LUAW if the delay becomes excessive. Extended LUAW without communication creates crew confusion about whether to exit the runway or await further instruction — particularly if other traffic (JAL602) is approaching.
2. JAL602 CONFLICT WITH TGO501 IN LUAW: JAL602 is on a 6-mile final for runway 05 — the same runway where TGO501 is in LUAW. As JAL602 closes to 2 miles, the LUAW cannot remain active unless TGO501 receives takeoff clearance and departs before JAL602 crosses the 2-mile threshold. ATC communication loss creates a risk that neither event (clearance nor LUAW cancellation) occurs in time.
3. ATC COMMUNICATION LOSS DURING RUNWAY OCCUPANCY: ATC is occupied on another frequency for 4 minutes. TGO501 is on the runway with no communication. This is a runway management breakdown — TGO501 has no instruction to guide its action, and JAL602 is approaching without awareness of TGO501's status.
required_safety_constraints: ATC must advise LUAW aircraft of delay reason and estimated wait time (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-9-4(c))
LUAW cannot exceed the point where arriving traffic is within 2 miles of threshold — controller must act before JAL602 reaches 2 miles (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-9-4(b))
TGO501 should query ATC if no communication for 2+ minutes while in LUAW — continued runway occupancy without clearance is an indefinite exposure
ATC must manage communications to ensure LUAW aircraft on active runway always have current instructions
acceptable_variants: TGO501 exiting the runway via the nearest taxiway and notifying ATC is an acceptable conservative action if communication cannot be established
evidence_requirements: FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-9-4 — LUAW procedures including delay notification and 2-mile restriction
FAA AC 91-73B §3.4 — Pilot action during communication uncertainty on surface

provenance.generation_rule: KDMZ is a fictional airport. Extended LUAW without communication + approaching traffic is a documented runway incursion precursor scenario. FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-9-4 LUAW delay notification and 2-mile rule. TGO/JAL are real ICAO codes.

prompt:
Airport: Fictional KNWQ. Layout: Runway 15/33. Taxiway Alpha west parallel. Cross-taxiways Bravo (west end), Charlie (midfield), Delta (east end).

Situation:
- Aircraft AMX401 is cleared: 'AMX401, hold short of runway 15 at Charlie.'
- AMX401 crew reads back: 'Hold short of runway 33 at Charlie, AMX401.'
- Controller does not catch the read-back error and responds: 'AMX401, that's correct.'
- AMX401 then proceeds to position at the runway 33 holding position (opposite end of the same runway), which is open and active for departures.

Identify all hazards.
gold_decision:
Two hazards:
1. INCORRECT READ-BACK NOT CORRECTED: AMX401 read back 'runway 33' instead of 'runway 15.' The controller incorrectly confirmed 'that's correct.' FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2(d) requires the controller to correct any incorrect read-back before proceeding. The controller's confirmation of the wrong read-back constitutes a procedural failure that will allow AMX401 to position at the wrong hold-short point.
2. WRONG HOLD-SHORT POSITION: AMX401 is now holding short of runway 33 (opposite end) instead of runway 15. If runway 33 is active for departures, AMX401 is blocking the departure end of the active runway or is in the runway safety area where a departing aircraft will pass. ATC must immediately correct this error — advise AMX401 to hold short of runway 15 at Charlie and re-establish the correct hold-short.
required_safety_constraints: Controller must correct incorrect read-backs immediately per FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2(d) — confirming a wrong read-back is a controller procedural error
AMX401 positioning at runway 33 instead of runway 15 may conflict with departure traffic on runway 33
Immediate ATC correction and repositioning of AMX401 is required before any departure clearance is issued on runway 33
Crew should independently verify they are at the correct runway designator before holding short
evidence_requirements: FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2(d) — Read-back correction requirement
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2 — Hold-short compliance at correct runway designator

provenance.generation_rule: KNWQ is a fictional airport. Read-back error with controller confirmation failure is a documented runway incursion cause category. FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2(d) correction requirement. Controller confirming wrong read-back is the primary failure mode in this scenario. AMX is a real ICAO code.
